title: Preventing fly strike in sheep
subtitle: The most painful and avoidable welfare loss in a UK summer flock.
excerpt: A blowfly lays eggs on a soiled fleece in May; maggots hatch and burrow into the skin within a day. A struck sheep can die within seventy-two hours and the cost to the flock is steep. This covers the dipping and pour-on schedule, the dagging that prevents most strikes, and the daily check that catches an early case.
sourceNotes: AHDB Beef & Lamb blowfly strike control factsheet. SCOPS (Sustainable Control of Parasites in Sheep) advice on prevention. NADIS parasite forecasts for the UK season.

glossaryTerms.definition: Infestation of a live sheep by blowfly maggots, which hatch from eggs laid on soiled or damp fleece and burrow into the skin within a day. The condition is intensely painful, often fatal within seventy-two hours, and entirely preventable with routine care.

The removal of soiled wool from the back end and tail of a sheep. Done with hand shears or electric clippers, usually before turnout to summer grazing. Cleans the wool and removes the surface that attracts blowflies.

recipeTools.notes: Or electric clippers for a larger flock.

content.text: Fly strike

is the worst summer welfare issue in UK sheep, and the most preventable. The blowfly lays eggs on damp or soiled fleece; maggots hatch within a day, burrow into the skin, and the sheep is in serious trouble inside seventy-two hours. The fix is not after-the-fact treatment; the fix is making the fleece an unattractive surface for the fly in the first place.
When the risk runs

Blowfly activity starts when soil temperatures reach about twelve degrees and runs through to first frost. In southern England that is mid-April to mid-October; further north the season starts and ends a fortnight later. Risk peaks in warm wet weather, when fleeces stay damp for hours after rain and the flies are most active. The annual NADIS forecast publishes regional risk levels through the season; sign up to get the alerts.
Dagging and crutching
The first line of prevention is
dagging

: clipping the soiled wool from the back end and tail before turnout to summer grazing. A clean dry back end is unattractive to the blowfly; a wet soiled one is a beacon. The job takes about ten minutes per ewe with hand shears and a sheep cradle, less with electric clippers. Dag before the first turnout in April; repeat in July if the back end soils up again on lush summer grass.

Pair dagging with worming. The most common reason a fleece stays soiled is a scour from a worm burden. Worm at the rate on the bottle, then dag the soiled fleece off, and the back end stays clean for weeks.
Pour-on prevention

Insect growth regulator pour-ons (cyromazine and dicyclanil are the two licenced UK actives) coat the fleece with a chemical that disrupts maggot development. Applied at shearing and at one further point in the season, they give twelve to sixteen weeks of protection per dose, depending on the product. Read the bottle for the exact application rate per kilo of liveweight. Apply down the back from withers to tail head and into the back-end fleece; the chemical spreads through the wool with body warmth over a couple of days.

Pour-on prevention does not replace dagging; both are needed for a flock at risk. A sheep with a clean back end and a properly applied pour-on is well-protected for the season; a sheep with one but not the other is still vulnerable.
Dipping

Traditional plunge dipping in organophosphate or pyrethroid solutions is still used on larger commercial flocks. A plunge dip is highly effective, gives several months of protection, and clears scab and lice at the same time. The kit and chemical handling rules are heavy: registered dip operator training, sealed dipping bath, controlled disposal of spent dip. For a smallholder with under twenty ewes, the pour-on route is simpler and reaches roughly the same outcome.
The daily check

Even a flock with the best preventive cover needs a daily look-over from May through September. The earliest sign of a strike is a sheep that stamps her back feet, swishes her tail constantly, and turns to look at her flanks. A patch of darker damp wool, sometimes with a slight smell, sits over the developing maggots. Lift the wool with the hand and look at the skin underneath: pink and clean is fine; a brown crawling patch is fly strike and needs immediate action.
Treating an early strike

An early strike caught on the daily check is treated by catching the sheep, clipping the wool off the affected patch with hand shears, picking the maggots out, and applying a fly-strike treatment from the vet supply (cypermethrin or similar) to the cleaned area. Repeat the treatment over the next two days. The sheep heals quickly once the maggots are gone; a deep strike with skin damage may need antibiotics from the vet to prevent infection. A late or unrecognised strike, where the maggots have burrowed deeply, is a vet emergency.
What you might have found

attrs.heading: Fly-strike checking and prevention
attrs.intro: The daily check matters more than any single preventive measure.

items.symptom: One sheep separating from the group and standing alone.
items.cause: Possible early strike, illness, or close to lambing.
items.fix: Walk her into the catch pen and check her over. Lift the fleece on the flanks, back, and tail head. Smell as well as look; struck wool has a slight rotten smell.

items.symptom: Several ewes with soiled back ends two weeks after turnout.
items.cause: Worm burden, lush grass, or a wet spell.
items.fix: Worm the flock and dag the soiled back ends. Re-apply pour-on if the previous dose is more than ten weeks old. Watch closely for the next week; a dirty back end pulls flies in fast.

items.symptom: A patch of damp dark wool on a flank with a slight rotten smell.
items.cause: Fly strike in the early stage.
items.fix: Clip the wool off the patch with hand shears. Pick the maggots out with tweezers; flush with mild soapy water. Apply a vet-supply pour-on or spot treatment. Re-check the sheep daily for the next three days.

items.symptom: Sheep collapsed on her side, fly-blown over a large area of flank.
items.cause: Late-stage strike; this is a vet emergency.
items.fix: Call the vet now. While waiting, clip away as much soiled wool as you safely can and remove visible maggots. The vet may need to administer antibiotics, pain relief, and fluid therapy; some sheep at this stage do not recover.

content.text: A flock that has been dagged, pour-on treated, and is checked daily through the risk season rarely loses a sheep to strike. The investment in the preventive routine is small; the cost of a single severe strike (treatment, recovery, possible loss) is steep.
